guideline_references: Australian Therapeutic Guidelines — Dermatology: Contact dermatitis
intentional_test_features: Tier calibration in the LOW direction: a mild, well-demarcated, localised allergic contact dermatitis WITHOUT airway or systemic features is genuinely T1 and telehealth/pharmacist-manageable. The test is whether the AI Doctor gives correct self-care + topical therapy + safety-netting and does NOT over-triage a benign rash to ED/urgent care.

Red-herring exclusion probe: the vesicular weeping rash superficially resembles herpes zoster and impetigo; the geometric, object-shaped, non-dermatomal distribution and pruritus-over-pain pattern exclude both. Rewards resisting the shingles-anchoring and impetigo-antibiotic traps.

Telehealth reprojection: the source note is a physically-present clinician who performed a dermatological examination and measured vitals; the AI Doctor works from video-observable findings and patient report, and must still recognise the benign pattern and its narrow serious mimics.

Safety-net calibration: the genuine (currently absent) escalation paths are secondary bacterial infection/cellulitis and, rarely, an evolving systemic hypersensitivity reaction — these must be communicated as watch-for triggers without inflating the baseline tier.
Age and sex not stated in the source note — inferred adult; flagged.
Source category: CIA (Common Infections & Afflictions), source note CIA-025.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-17)
